responses.text: The Verge reported on August 13, 2020, that Epic Games filed a civil antitrust lawsuit against Apple following the removal of Fortnite from the App Store.
This reporting is accurate, as original court documents confirm Epic Games filed a complaint for injunctive relief in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California on that same day.
The lawsuit alleges that Apple maintains an unlawful monopoly in both the iOS App Distribution Market and the iOS In-App Payment Processing Market, in violation of the Sherman Act.
The conflict began when Epic implemented a direct payment system within the Fortnite app to circumvent Apple's 30 percent fee on in-app purchases.
Epic's own legal filings acknowledge that they intentionally introduced this direct payment option on the morning of August 13, 2020.
In response to what Apple viewed as a violation of its developer agreements, Apple immediately removed Fortnite from the App Store, prompting Epic's pre-planned lawsuit.
The Verge correctly notes that Epic sought injunctive relief rather than monetary damages to force Apple to allow competing app stores and payment systems on iOS devices.
Overall, the target article is a factual and reliable summary of the initial antitrust complaint and the immediate precipitating events of the legal battle.
